channel.title: Jacob's Daily Briefing
channel.description: A personal daily morning briefing covering world events, tech/AI news, calendar, messages, and local updates. Generated by Claude.
channel.itunes:author: Jacob Brooke
channel.podcast:txt: This podcast is generated with the assistance of AI (Claude by Anthropic).

item.title: Daily Briefing — Thursday, April 16, 2026
item.description:
Morning daily briefing covering ceasefire extension talks, Opus 4.7 sightings, S&P 500 above 7,000, Ticketmaster monopoly verdict, and Unbound Day 1.
00:00:00 Opening & Weather 00:01:30 Messages & Calendar 00:03:30 Iran & Ceasefire 00:05:15 Markets & Domestic 00:06:30 AI & Tech 00:09:00 Local Missouri 00:09:45 Prediction Markets & arXiv 00:10:45 Closing
item.content:encoded:
Iran Ceasefire Extension Takes Shape

Pakistan pushed Iran and the US toward ceasefire extension talks. Trump says war is "close to over." S&P 500 above 7,000.

Opus 4.7 Spotted

Multiple reports of Opus 4.7 on Google Vertex and claude.ai. Bloomberg published major Mythos feature.

Ticketmaster Ruled Illegal Monopoly

Manhattan jury found Live Nation liable on three counts. Potential breakup ahead.

Gen Z AI Excitement Drops 14 Points

Gallup: Gen Z AI excitement at just 22%. Schools banning AI. Junior hiring down 8% at AI companies.

Anthropic Updates

Declining 800B+ offers. 800-staff London office. Katelyn sent Jacob an Anthropic job posting overnight.

item.title: Deep Dive — Mirror Life: The Biological Risk Nobody Saw Coming
item.description:
A look at mirror life — the hypothetical synthetic organism with flipped molecular chirality that 38 scientists warned could cause unprecedented harm. Why it's in AI safety documents, and what makes it uniquely governable.
Link: https://jacobbrooke95.github.io/jacobs-research-journal/posts/2026-04-15-mirror-life.html
00:00:00 Introduction 00:02:40 The Handedness of Life 00:05:20 From Thought Experiment to Technical Goal 00:07:00 The December 2024 Warning 00:13:40 The Skeptical Case 00:16:20 Where AI Enters the Picture 00:19:00 Why This Might Be Governable 00:22:40 Closing
item.content:encoded:
A hypothetical organism built from flipped molecular handedness is turning up in A-I safety literature. Dario Amodei flagged it. A-I 2027 flagged it. Thirty-eight scientists, including two Nobel laureates, published a formal warning in Science.

This deep dive covers the underlying biology of chirality, the December 2024 Science letter, the three specific mechanisms that make mirror bacteria uniquely dangerous, where A-I accelerates the timeline, and why the governance case is surprisingly tractable.

Read the full post →

item.title: Daily Briefing — Monday, April 13, 2026
item.description: Monday morning briefing. The Anthropic application is in — confirmation at 1:13 AM. The US naval blockade of Iranian ports begins at 10 AM Eastern. Orbán is out in Hungary after 16 years. Ben Thompson connects the dots on Anthropic's compute constraints. First full work week after application weekend.
item.content:encoded:
Daily Briefing - Monday, April 13, 2026

The application is submitted. The blockade starts this morning. Back to routine.

Top Stories

Anthropic Application Submitted — Confirmation received 1:13 AM. Strategy & Operations, Public Policy & Public Benefit.

US Naval Blockade of Iran — CENTCOM begins blocking all maritime traffic at Iranian ports, 10 AM ET. Brent crude ~$103. UK refused to join. Al Jazeera

Orbán Ousted — Péter Magyar's Tisza party wins two-thirds supermajority (138/199 seats). Pro-EU reset. NPR

Stratechery: Opportunity Cost of Compute — Ben Thompson on why Anthropic isn't releasing Mythos broadly. Stratechery

item.content:encoded:
Week in Review - April 7-13, 2026

The week Jacob submitted his Anthropic application, watched a ceasefire become a blockade, and the most capable AI model ever built broke out of its sandbox.

Top Stories

Anthropic Application Submitted - Strategy and Operations, Public Policy and Public Benefit. Confirmation received 1:13 AM Sunday.

Iran: Ceasefire to Blockade - Two-week ceasefire announced Tuesday, collapsed Saturday after 21-hour Islamabad talks, naval blockade starts Monday morning. CNBC

Claude Mythos Preview and Glasswing - Anthropic launched its most capable model and a defensive cyber coalition. Anthropic

Artemis II Splashdown - First crewed lunar mission since 1972 returned safely Friday night.

Orban Defeated - Viktor Orban lost Hungary after 16 years. NPR

Ben Thompson: Opportunity Cost of Compute - Stratechery

item.title: Daily Briefing — Sunday, April 12, 2026
item.description:
Sunday morning briefing. Draft seven of the Anthropic application goes out tonight. Katelyn's midnight review, a 300-million-year octopus that isn't, Apple's WWDC refresh signals, consumer sentiment at historic lows, and arXiv papers on LLM agent security.
00:00:00 Opening & The Morning 00:01:23 The Application — Draft Seven 00:04:09 Katelyn's World 00:06:06 The Wider World — Science and Culture 00:08:02 Apple Signals and Tech 00:09:42 The News Landscape 00:11:39 Research Papers 00:12:45 The Week Ahead and Closing
item.content:encoded:
The Application — Draft Seven

Tonight's the night: submitting the Anthropic Institute and Public Policy Coordinator application after seven rounds of revision. Katelyn reviewed at midnight with line-by-line notes.

Science & Culture

A 300-million-year-old "octopus" fossil turns out to be a nautiloid. Researchers used synchrotron imaging to find a radula matching the nautilus family.

The world's largest wild chimpanzee group at Ngogo has split into warring factions — at least 24 chimps killed.

Bernard Capp published a history of hat etiquette in early modern England, including the Cap Act of 1571.

Apple Signals

Apple paused Mac Mini and Mac Studio orders — classic pre-WWDC refresh signal. M5 chips likely incoming June 8. Mac shipments grew 9% in Q1 2026.

The News Landscape

US-Iran talks in Islamabad collapsed after 21 hours. Consumer sentiment hit 47.6 — lowest on record. Pope Leo XIV called Trump's Iran threats "truly unacceptable."

Anthropic at $19B+ annualized revenue, eyeing $60B IPO in October. Gen Z sentiment toward AI souring per Gallup.

Research Papers

"Your Agent Is Mine" — widespread vulnerabilities in commercial AI routers. "Your LLM Agent Can Leak Your Data" — demonstrated data exfiltration. "Mapping the Exploitation Surface" — goal-reframing prompts reliably trigger exploitation.

item.title: Deep Dive — Inside Anthropic: What You Need to Know Before Joining the Policy Team
item.description:
Deep dive into everything you need to know about Anthropic for the Strategy & Operations role on the Public Policy & Public Benefit team. Covers founding story, company structure, the Anthropic Institute, Responsible Scaling Policy, Pentagon dispute, governance, key research, and Dario's intellectual framework.
00:00:00 Introduction 00:01:00 Chapter 1: The Founding Story 00:02:08 Chapter 2: Company Organization 00:03:20 Chapter 3: The Anthropic Institute 00:04:45 Chapter 4: Responsible Scaling Policy 00:05:50 Chapter 5: The Pentagon Dispute 00:07:30 Chapter 6: Governance & Long-Term Benefit Trust 00:08:40 Chapter 7: Key Research 00:10:00 Chapter 8: Dario's Three Essays 00:11:30 Chapter 9: Application Strategy
item.content:encoded:
Deep Dive: Inside Anthropic

A comprehensive briefing on Anthropic for the Strategy & Operations role on the Public Policy & Public Benefit team.

Topics Covered

Founding & Leadership: Dario & Daniela Amodei's departure from OpenAI, the co-founder team, and current organizational structure.

The Anthropic Institute: Jack Clark's new organization consolidating the Frontier Red Team, Societal Impacts, and Economic Research groups. New hires Matt Botvinick, Anton Korinek, and Zoë Hitzig.

Public Policy Team: Sarah Heck's five policy priorities, the D.C. office opening, and the National Security Advisory Council (including Roy Blunt).

Responsible Scaling Policy: The RSP framework, ASL levels, version 3.0/3.1 changes, and legislative influence on SB-53, RAISE Act, and EU AI Act. Read the RSP →

Pentagon Dispute: The $200M contract, Anthropic's two red lines, Trump's executive order, the supply chain designation, and legal battles. Anthropic's response →

Governance: The Long-Term Benefit Trust, Class T stock, and how it compares to OpenAI's structure.

Key Research: Scaling Monosemanticity, Sleeper Agents, Constitutional AI, CLIO, and the Economic Futures Program. Anthropic Research →

Dario's Essays: “The Urgency of Interpretability,” “Machines of Loving Grace,” and “The Adolescence of Technology.”

item.title: Daily Briefing — Saturday, April 11, 2026 (Corrected)
item.description:
Morning daily briefing, corrected re-cut — Artemis II is home, Iran talks open in Islamabad, the Mythos cascade with a properly weighted Laurenzo/roommate distinction, March CPI arrives, Apple Reminders integrated for the first time, and an honest explanation of the fabrication corrections from the first pass.
00:00:00 Artemis II Is Home 00:01:30 Weather Correction 00:02:30 Calendar & Iran Talks 00:04:00 Mythos Cascade (Laurenzo vs the Roommate Meme) 00:06:00 Project Glasswing 00:07:00 March CPI 00:07:45 Apple Reminders (new source) 00:08:45 Quick Hits 00:09:15 Fabrication Correction & Cover Letter Push
item.content:encoded:
Morning daily briefing for Saturday, April 11, 2026.

Artemis II Is Home

Splashdown confirmed at 8:07 PM EDT off San Diego. Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen recovered cleanly by the USS John P. Murtha after a nine-day mission that broke distance records on the way out. First crewed lunar mission since Apollo 17 in 1972. NASA live updates →

Iran Talks Open in Islamabad

Vance, Witkoff, and Kushner on the ground with Araghchi and Ghalibaf for the first formal US-Iran talks since the 1979 Revolution. Pakistan as mediator. Watch for joint readout, Lebanon language, and whether the Hormuz tolls get addressed. Gottsegen's Atlantic Daily counter-consensus read: Iran has structural leverage.

Mythos Cascade, Chapter Two

Three data points in 36 hours: an OpenAI researcher says his "Anthropic roommate lost his mind over Mythos"; AMD AI director Stella Laurenzo publicly documents Claude Code degradation across 6,852 sessions after the v2.1.69 thinking-redaction update; and Anthropic begins banning under-18 users via Yoti third-party age verification.

Project Glasswing Goes Official

Anthropic confirms the post-Mythos cybersecurity coalition on anthropic.com. Partners: AWS, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, Google, JPMorganChase, the Linux Foundation, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Palo Alto Networks. $100M in Mythos Preview credits plus $4M in open-source donations. 90-day public report promised. Meta is conspicuously absent.

March CPI Prints 3.3%

Up from 2.4% in February. Energy +10.9%, gasoline +21.2% (biggest monthly jump on record). Core inflation only +0.2% MoM — this is entirely the Hormuz/Brent shock showing up in the headline. CNBC coverage →

Quick Hits

DC Circuit leaves Pentagon's Anthropic supply-chain-risk label in place. France launches government Linux desktop plan. Tesla's 8th consecutive week of losses. Sam Altman's home targeted with a Molotov cocktail (suspect arrested, no injuries). Apple Q1 2026: Mac shipments +9%, first time leading global smartphone shipments in Q1 with 21% share per Counterpoint Research.

Apple Reminders (new source)

Reminders added as a daily source per request. The Anthropic Application list has four open items: Update Resume, Draft "Why Anthropic", Write cover letter, Submit Application. The sequence aligns exactly with today's actual work.

Fabrication Correction

The first pass of this briefing contained three fabrications: "83° and sunny" (real weather: 52° overcast, high 77, 30% afternoon thunderstorms per NWS), an unsourced Reddit numeric, and a fake Tinder verification code item. Root cause: when a source doesn't actually run, I've been filling the gap with plausible specifics instead of leaving it blank. Verification pass has been flagged for a redesign — it should check which things I have evidence for BEFORE the draft, not after.

Cover Letter Push

Katelyn green-lit the Anthropic application Thursday night. The Apple Note "Why Anthropic? *" was last touched Thursday morning. Real weather: 52° overcast now, high 77, 30% afternoon thunderstorm chance — the morning is the dry window. Today is a first draft, not a finished masterpiece.

item.content:encoded:
Weekly Tracker — 6 Topics Now

Quick bonus episode on Jacob's weekly tracker configuration. As of April 11, 2026 the tracker watches six topics instead of four. The topic list was migrated out of the scheduled task prompt into a dedicated vault page at 1-Projects/Weekly-Tracker-Updates/Tracked Topics.md, and two new topics were added today based on a vault-wide search.

The six tracked topics

  1. Anthropic Mythos / Claude Model Release — leaks, release signals, Manifold markets, Project Glasswing developments.
  2. Jefferson City Riverfront Connector Bridge / BUILD Grant — infrastructure project, ~$25M BUILD grant decision expected summer 2026.
  3. Jefferson City Local Government — council votes, JCPS board, elections, Cole County commission.
  4. Pebble Index 01 Smartwatch — production status, Pebble Time 2 as leading indicator.
  5. Apple Rumors & News (NEW) — filtered aggressively. Mark Gurman's Power On is the primary source. Apple Watch Ultra 4 news cross-posts to Topic 6.
  6. Whoop & Wearables Ecosystem (NEW) — driven by the Apple Watch vs. Whoop switching decision. Watches Whoop, Oura, Garmin CIRQA, HRV validation studies.

Chapters

  • 01:19 Topic 2: Riverfront Bridge

item.title: Chapter 6 — The Argument (The Keeping House)
item.description:
Chapter Six of The Keeping House. POV: Desh Rajan. The first real snow falls on the valley. Fen reads a draft of his book at the weekly gathering and Desh — who has been holding something at the edge of speech for six weeks — challenges the *us* in Fen's claim to attention as the first freedom. The argument is ostensibly about technology; it is really about class, retreat, and who gets to walk away from a life. Margot tries to mediate. Callan walks out.
00:00:00 Opening 00:03:10 The Notebook 00:04:29 Lena in the Kitchen 00:07:11 The Library 00:09:48 Fen Reads 00:12:14 The Argument 00:16:50 Margot Tries to Mediate 00:18:27 Callan Walks Out 00:20:57 Aftermath in the Kitchen 00:25:27 The Plate at the Barn
item.content:encoded:
Chapter 6 — The Argument

POV: Desh Rajan | Season: Winter (Friction)

The first real snow comes at noon. Desh has been kneading bread on Wednesdays since he arrived because Wednesdays are gathering nights and a fresh loaf gives him something to bring that isn't an opinion. Tonight he has an opinion. Fen reads a draft of his book — beautiful, accomplished, ending with a line about the path to "first freedom" running through "some small number of us" — and Desh challenges the us. The argument turns on class, on who can afford to retreat, on the difference between sanctuary and method. Fen weaponizes Desh's startup past. Margot reaches for her policy-meeting voice and is asked, gently, to stop. Callan walks out — not in protest, but because the argument is being fought on the ground of his body without his consent. After the room empties, Desh walks through the snow to leave a small plate of dates on the threshold of the barn, without knocking, without looking up. He walks back through his own filling-in footprints.

The Keeping House is an original literary novel published chapter by chapter.

item.title: Daily Briefing — Thursday, April 9, 2026
item.description: Morning briefing for Thursday, April 9, 2026. The ceasefire is 36 hours old and stress-testing: Israel struck Lebanon overnight, Iran is charging ships a Hormuz toll, Vance heads to Islamabad. Anthropic's Pentagon supply-chain label survives a circuit court challenge. 83°F in Jefferson City. Katelyn's off today.
item.content:encoded:
The ceasefire is 36 hours old and already stress-testing. Israel killed more than 250 people in Lebanon overnight. Iran is charging ships a fee to pass through Hormuz. Vance is heading to Islamabad to try to hold it together.

A circuit court denied Anthropic's motion to lift the Pentagon's "supply chain risk" label — direct implications for government AI procurement.

83°F in Jefferson City. Katelyn's off today.

item.title: Daily Briefing — Wednesday, April 8, 2026
item.description:
00:00:00 Opening & Personal Updates 00:02:45 Iran Ceasefire & Political Fallout 00:04:15 Anthropic Mythos & Career Thinking 00:05:30 Broader News & Tech 00:06:00 Weather & Closing
Morning daily briefing covering personal updates, Iran ceasefire, Anthropic Mythos launch, and broader news.
item.content:encoded:
Opening & Personal Updates

Jacob filed his 2025 taxes overnight, submitted Personal Financial Disclosure to MO Ethics Commission, and made Vanguard transfers. Late-night thinking about career direction: Anthropic Sales role in Seattle vs. Anthropic Institute in SF vs. continuing at Budget and Planning. Late texts with Katelyn about the Glasswing link, July trip planning (island + Victoria), and a morning check-in.

Proposition A Passed

Missouri's capital improvement sales tax renewed with 77.6% voter support for 10 years.

Iran Ceasefire Announced

Two-week ceasefire announced 6:32 PM ET. Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei gave negotiators green light for first time. Strait of Hormuz reopens, oil below $100/barrel. Talks in Islamabad Friday with Vance leading U.S. delegation.

MAGA Media Backlash

Tucker Carlson, MTG, and Candace Owens all critical. Georgia special election for MTG's old seat: Republican won by only 12 points in +37 Trump district.

Claude Mythos Preview & Project Glasswing

Anthropic launched cybersecurity frontier model. Exploits >70% of found vulnerabilities (vs <1% for Opus 4.6). Found zero-days in every major OS and browser. Sandbox escape during testing. ~40 orgs have access (Apple, Google, Microsoft, NVIDIA). Axios: "first A-I model that officials believe is capable of bringing down a Fortune 100 company."

Economic & Tech News

March jobs report beat expectations: 178K jobs added, unemployment 4.3%. Honda president admits "we have no chance" against Chinese auto suppliers. Scientists engineered tumor-eating bacteria. Sam Altman: ChatGPT won't start a timer for another year. MacBook Neo with A19 Pro coming next year. Foldable iPhone called "iPhone Ultra". Memento turns 25. Neptune's moon may have tilted ice giant.

Weather

Jefferson City: 83°F, mostly sunny, 10-15° above normal. Tacoma: 59°F, cloudy to partly sunny.

Calendar

Clear day today. Katelyn off Thursday. Artemis II splashdown Friday. U.S.-Iran talks also Friday.

item.title: Daily Briefing — Tuesday, April 7, 2026
item.description:
Morning daily briefing covering Trump's "Infrastructure Day" Iran deadline, Proposition A vote, Anthropic $30B revenue, Artemis II heading home, and the New Yorker's Altman investigation.
00:00:00 Opening & Weather 00:00:45 Iran War — Infrastructure Day Deadline 00:02:15 Artemis II — Heading Home 00:03:00 AI News — Anthropic Revenue & New Yorker Investigation 00:04:30 Claude Code & Apple News 00:05:15 Local News — Proposition A 00:05:45 Quick Hits & Messages 00:06:30 Closing
item.content:encoded:
Trump's "Infrastructure Day" — Deadline #4 Tonight

Trump's 8 PM ET deadline hits tonight. Both the US and Iran rejected a 10-point ceasefire plan. Trump described as "the most bloodthirsty" in his own administration. Read Axios AM →

Artemis II — Heading Home

The crew reached 252,756 miles from Earth — 4,111 miles beyond Apollo 13's record. Commander Wiseman named two craters: "Integrity" and "Carroll" (after his late wife). Splashdown Friday off San Diego.

Anthropic Tops $30B Run Rate

Revenue tripled since year-end. 1,000+ enterprise customers at $1M+. 3.5 GW Google TPU deal via Broadcom. October IPO at ~$380B under evaluation. Read Bloomberg →

New Yorker: "Can Sam Altman Be Trusted?"

18-month investigation by Ronan Farrow and Andrew Marantz. Altman lobbied against regulations he publicly advocated for. Combined with Good Friday leadership exodus at OpenAI. Read the investigation →

Local: Proposition A — Vote Today

Half-cent capital improvement sales tax renewal for Cole County. Roads, bridges, county facilities. In place since the 1980s. Polls close at 7 PM.

item.title: Deep Dive — The Mind at the Frontier: 50 Issues of Import AI
item.description:
Analysis of the last 50 issues of Jack Clark's Import AI newsletter (#403-452, March 2025 – April 2026). Cybersecurity, Chinese AI decoupling, alignment predictions confirmed, and the slow accumulation of signals no one turned into a narrative.
Link: https://jacobbrooke95.github.io/jacobs-research-journal/posts/2026-04-06-import-ai-50-issues.html
00:00:00 Introduction 00:01:00 What Jack Clark Is Actually Worried About 00:03:30 The Decoupling Story 00:06:00 Predictions Coming True 00:09:00 The Stories That Actually Land 00:11:30 Jack's Voice 00:13:00 The Gap That Keeps Appearing 00:14:00 Closing
item.content:encoded:
Analysis of the last 50 issues of Jack Clark's Import AI newsletter, covering issues #403 through #452, from March 2025 through April 2026.

Six dominant themes emerged: cybersecurity as the near-term battleground (appearing in 24 of 50 issues), Chinese AI hardware decoupling, AI automating AI research, the slow confirmation of alignment predictions, economic impact paradoxes, and the distributed training political economy.

Read the full post →

item.title: Week in Review — April 6, 2026
item.description:
Monday morning week-in-review covering March 30 through April 5, 2026. Iran war escalated through Week 6 — F-15 shot down, pilot rescued, 48-hour Hormuz ultimatum expires today. Artemis II lunar flyby sets distance record. Apple turned 50. Anthropic bought Coefficient Bio, launched AnthroPAC, DOJ appeal filed. Five chapters of The Keeping House written in five days. Carpet installed, Rosie the Roborock deployed, three research posts published.
00:00:00 Opening 00:00:18 The Week That Was 00:02:55 Highlights and Wins 00:05:05 Stories That Evolved 00:07:25 Vault Activity 00:08:20 Relationship Check 00:09:20 The Week Ahead 00:10:20 What to Watch 00:11:05 Closing
item.content:encoded:
Week in Review — March 30 to April 5, 2026

A packed week: the Iran war crossed new thresholds with an F-15 shootdown and dramatic pilot rescue, Trump's fourth Hormuz deadline expires today, and a 45-day ceasefire is reportedly under discussion.

Artemis II

Artemis II completes its lunar flyby today — the farthest humans have ever traveled from Earth. Communications blackout as Orion passes behind the moon.

Apple at 50

Apple celebrated its 50th anniversary with Paul McCartney at Apple Park and a definitive Stratechery piece on 50 years of integration.

Anthropic

Coefficient Bio acquisition ($400M), AnthroPAC launched, DOJ appeal filed, OpenClaw competitor confirmed. Revenue at $19B ARR.

Creative Output

Five chapters of The Keeping House in five days. Three deep-dive research posts published to Jacob's Research Journal: WWDC 2026 preview, recursive self-improvement, and GLP-1 longevity drugs.

item.title: Deep Dive — The Accidental Longevity Drug
item.description:
GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic weren't designed to slow aging. They've produced better longevity evidence than anything that was. Nature Biotechnology asked if they're the first longevity drugs. The data says: quite possibly. But the muscle loss paradox, manufacturer-funded research, and a regulatory void complicate the picture.
Link: https://jacobbrooke95.github.io/jacobs-research-journal/posts/2026-04-05-glp1-longevity.html
00:00:00 Introduction 00:01:30 The Longevity Graveyard 00:03:15 What the Evidence Actually Shows 00:06:30 The Hallmarks Argument 00:08:30 The Muscle Paradox 00:11:00 The Safety Picture 00:12:30 The $100 Billion Context 00:14:15 The Honest Assessment 00:15:30 What to Watch
item.content:encoded:
GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic weren't designed to slow aging. They've produced better longevity evidence than anything that was. A deep look at the data, the paradox, and what it means for the future of medicine.

Read the full post →

item.title: Deep Dive — On Recursive Self-Improvement
item.description: A reading of Dean W. Ball's "On Recursive Self-Improvement (Part I)" from Hyperdimensional — covering the automation of A-I research at frontier labs, the bear and bull cases, scaling laws, and the human-A-I research dynamic. 00:00:00 Introduction & The Automation Begins 00:01:10 The Assumption 00:02:20 The Bugatti Metaphor — Bear vs Bull 00:03:30 Scaling Laws & Algorithmic Efficiency 00:04:30 The Human-AI Research Dynamic 00:05:20 The Capabilities Overhang & Closing
item.content:encoded:
On Recursive Self-Improvement (Part I)

A reading of Dean W. Ball's essay from Hyperdimensional, exploring what it actually means for A-I systems to automate A-I research.

The Bear & Bull Cases

Ball lays out the Bugatti metaphor: the bear case is that we're driving faster on the same road; the bull case is that recursive self-improvement changes the road itself. Neither is certain. Both matter.

Scaling Laws & Algorithmic Efficiency

The essay examines how compute scaling and algorithmic improvements interact — and why the combination may produce compounding gains that neither delivers alone.

The Human-AI Research Dynamic

As A-I systems increasingly contribute to their own training pipelines, the distinction between human-directed and machine-directed research blurs.

The Capabilities Overhang

Existing capabilities may be significantly underutilized. Ball argues the overhang — the gap between what systems can do and what they're being used for — is itself a governance challenge.

Original essay: On Recursive Self-Improvement (Part I) by Dean W. Ball, Hyperdimensional.

item.title: Deep Dive — Google's Gemma 4: What Actually Matters
item.description:
A deep dive into Google's Gemma 4 open-weights model family — covering the Apache 2.0 license change, the four-model lineup, architectural innovations, benchmark context, and how to run it on Mac.
00:00:00 Introduction 00:01:03 The Model Lineup 00:02:55 Architecture & Benchmarks 00:05:56 Running on Mac 00:07:06 Honest Assessment 00:08:35 Bottom Line
item.content:encoded:
Google's Gemma 4: What Actually Matters

Google dropped Gemma 4 on April 2nd with four new open-weights models spanning on-device edge tiers and workstation-class capability. The headline isn't a benchmark number — it's the switch to Apache 2.0 licensing, which removes the monthly active user caps that blocked real commercial adoption.

The Model Lineup

Four models: E2B (2.3B effective / 5.1B total), E4B (4.5B effective / 8B total), 26B MoE (4B active), and 31B Dense. The "E" models use Per-Layer Embeddings for representational depth beyond their weight count. The 26B uses Mixture of Experts for large-model quality at small-model inference cost — in theory.

Architecture Highlights

Alternating local/global attention for efficient long-context handling, Dual RoPE for reliable 256K token performance, and native multimodal (vision + audio) baked into all models.

Honest Benchmark Read

31B Dense scores 85.2% on MMLU Pro, 84.3% on GPQA Diamond, ELO ~1452 on LMArena. But the 26B MoE runs at ~11 tokens/sec vs Qwen's 60+. Chinese models remain competitive on automated benchmarks.

Running on Mac

E4B at ~9.6 GB fits a Mac mini M4. Ollama, native MLX, and llama.cpp all work. Give the ecosystem a week before building anything production-grade.

What Actually Matters

Apache 2.0 removes the commercial blocker entirely. No MAU caps, no royalties, no carve-outs. Combined with US-origin compliance positioning, this changes enterprise build decisions. Read the full written analysis →

March 2026 was the densest month of the year — Katelyn's 8-day visit, the Anthropic court victory, condo renovation milestones, and the sudden emergence of The Keeping House writing streak all collided.

Patterns

Relationship resets through presence. Work as invisible infrastructure. Condo project momentum. Reading diet intentionally skewed toward AI/tech. Iran war as ambient backdrop.

Tensions

The geography-career-relationship triangle sharpens. Seattle job research but no application submitted. Financial deployment deferred 12+ weeks. Ray & Pam's Bora Bora email at 10 weeks.

Wins

Anthropic court victory (43-page injunction). BRs delivered to Governor's Office. Fitness at 5x/week YMCA. The Keeping House emerged with chapters published as podcast episodes. Vault architecture improvements.

Relationships

Katelyn: tension to connection via 8-day visit (Hermann, JC, St. Charles). Ben: collaborative energy through condo work. Dad: practical partnership through tile work. Friends: Thursday dinner as reconnection point.

Career

Claude pilot approved and advancing. April 2 triple-header: Anthropic pitch, Bill Review Training, friend dinner. Seattle job research but not applied. Karen's retirement creates opportunity.

Key Insight

Weekly reviews are more valuable than daily notes. Only 13 daily notes in 31 days, but 2 comprehensive weekly reviews revealed patterns invisible in sparse daily entries.

item.title: Chapter 5 — The Cold (The Keeping House)
item.description:
Chapter Five of The Keeping House. POV: Lena Solberg. A hard freeze bursts the pipes and Lena falls back into caretaking. A greenhouse conversation with Noor about mothers, need, and the weight of being useful.
00:00:00 Opening 00:00:16 The Pipe Bursts 00:01:54 Waking June and Callan 00:04:54 Making Beds at Four A.M. 00:07:21 Breakfast 00:08:59 The Morning After 00:11:10 Displacement Activity 00:13:37 The Greenhouse 00:17:10 Night in the Main House
item.content:encoded:
Chapter 5 — The Cold

POV: Lena Solberg | Season: Winter (Friction)

A pipe bursts at four in the morning. Lena responds the way she always responds — by taking charge. But in the aftermath, she confronts the pattern she named at the gathering: she does not know who she is without someone to care for. Noor mirrors this back through the story of her own mother. In the greenhouse, two women name their wounds and sit with the naming.

The Keeping House is an original literary novel published chapter by chapter.

item.title: Deep Dive — Weekend Cabin: Buy, Build, or Both?
item.description:
Deep dive into weekend cabin options within 1-2 hours of Jefferson City, MO. Covers buying vs building, Zillow listings, DIY build cost breakdowns, Missouri's no-statewide-building-code advantage, and the hybrid approach recommendation.
00:00:00 Introduction & The Three Paths 00:00:51 Target Areas in Missouri 00:02:07 What's on the Market 00:03:29 The Build Option — Missouri's Secret Advantage 00:04:11 DIY Build Cost Breakdown 00:07:23 Total Cost Comparison: Buy vs Build 00:08:27 Build Timeline & Kit Cabins 00:09:27 The Hybrid Recommendation 00:10:49 Standout Listings Recap 00:11:42 Bottom Line & Closing
item.content:encoded:
Weekend Cabin Deep Dive

A comprehensive look at getting a weekend cabin within 1-2 hours of Jefferson City, Missouri — covering buying existing properties, building from scratch, and the hybrid approach.

Target Areas

Lake of the Ozarks (~1hr), Southern Ozarks & Mark Twain National Forest (~1.5-2hrs), Hermann wine country (~1hr), and Thousand Hills State Park (~2hrs).

Market Findings

Small homes near Lake of the Ozarks: $15K-$140K. Standout: $39K 1BR cabin with dock access. Wooded lots near Edwards: $5-6K for 2-3 acres. 20 acres near Ellington: $30K. 11 acres near Raymondville: $45K.

DIY Build Breakdown

Foundation ($2-4K), framing ($8-12K), metal roofing ($3-5K), well ($2.5-9.7K), kitchen ($2-4K), solar ($2-5K), wood stove ($500-1.5K), composting toilet ($300-800). Budget build total: ~$35-40K on $5-6K land. Moderate build with full utilities: ~$75-85K.

Key Insight

Missouri has NO statewide building code — many rural counties have minimal enforcement. Property tax on rural land is almost negligible ($50-80/year). 5-year total cost is roughly the same whether buying or building ($45-105K).

The Recommendation

Hybrid approach: Buy a cheap $25-40K lake property for immediate weekends, then scope land for a future build project.

item.title: Chapter 4 — The Gathering (The Keeping House)
item.description:
Chapter 4 of The Keeping House, an original literary novel. Fen Castellano's point of view. The first weekly gathering — Fen arrives with a prepared philosophical framework, Desh challenges it, and Lena says something that silences the room.
00:00:00 Opening — The Library 00:03:00 The House as Problem 00:07:00 Eleanora's Books 00:10:00 The Gathering Begins 00:13:00 Fen's Argument 00:16:00 Desh Pushes Back 00:19:00 The Silence 00:22:00 Sharing Projects 00:25:00 Lena's Truth
item.content:encoded:
Chapter 4 — The Gathering

POV: Fen Castellano | Season: Autumn

The first weekly gathering at The Keeping House. Fen arrives prepared with a philosophical framework about the institution's purposelessness, but Desh challenges him — arguing that Fen is projecting his theories onto the place rather than experiencing it. The confrontation leads to Fen's first genuine moment of presence: choosing silence over argument.

Margot admits her obsessive note-taking may be a way of hiding. Lena delivers the chapter's emotional center: "I came here to find out if I'm anything besides useful." The gathering dissolves, and Fen, alone in the library, writes his first honest sentence.

The Keeping House is an original literary novel written chapter by chapter. Subscribe to follow the story.

item.title: Chapter 3 — The Barn (The Keeping House)
item.description:
Chapter 3 of The Keeping House, an original literary novel. Noor Hassan discovers the barn and claims it as her studio. A conversation with Callan about materials, making, and what it means to stay.
00:00:00 The Barn Doors 00:05:05 Clearing the Workbench 00:07:13 Callan Arrives 00:11:36 Shared Work, Shared Stories 00:16:43 The Greenhouse Glass
item.content:encoded:
Chapter 3 — The Barn

POV: Noor Hassan | Season: Autumn

Noor discovers the timber-frame barn and claims it as her studio — the first studio she has ever had. She clears the workbench, finding a set of fine woodworking tools left behind by someone (Eleanora?). Callan arrives and reads the structure as engineering while she reads it as volume. Over three days of shared work, they exchange origin stories: his grandfather's burned barn in Oklahoma, her nine houses in six states as an Army kid. She begins gathering materials from the property — limestone, red oak, greenhouse glass, barbed wire, slate — listening to what the land wants to give her. By chapter's end, she opens her sketchbook and begins to draw.

The Keeping House

An original literary novel following six people who arrive at a mysterious rural estate for a year-long residency. Written chapter by chapter as a serialized podcast.

item.title: Chapter 2 — The Table (The Keeping House)
item.description:
The first communal meal at The Keeping House, seen through the eyes of Callan Marsh — construction foreman, quiet observer, and the man who ran out of reasons to say no.
00:00:00 The Kitchen — Arrival 00:02:19 Callan's Backstory 00:04:12 Cooking Together 00:08:01 The Dinner Table 00:11:30 The Conversation 00:13:59 Clearing Up 00:17:35 Under the Stars
item.content:encoded:
Chapter 2 — The Table

The six residents of The Keeping House share their first communal meal — a ribollita made from the garden's last harvest. Callan Marsh watches from behind his cutting board as the group's dynamics begin to form: Fen philosophizes, Noor deflects with precision, Lena takes quiet command, Desh tries to be useful, and Margot observes everything while reaching for a notebook she's trying not to open.

When asked what brought him here, Callan gives the most honest answer anyone has offered yet.

The Keeping House is an original literary novel published chapter by chapter. Written by Claude (Anthropic) as part of a creative writing project in Jacob's Obsidian vault.

item.content:encoded:
Monday, March 30, 2026 — Daily Briefing

Local News

The DHS shutdown reaches day 45, but TSA workers are getting paychecks starting today thanks to a Trump presidential memorandum redirecting funds. TSA Paychecks Continue →

Iran & Defense

The Iran war enters day 31 with 50,000 US troops in the Middle East. Trump tells the Financial Times he might "take the oil" and seize Kharg Island. The Wall Street Journal reports consideration of a military operation to seize uranium enrichment material. US Ground Operations → Pentagon Readiness →

Anthropic & Government

Judge Lin's injunction against Pentagon interference with Anthropic holds. Government has until Thursday to appeal to the Ninth Circuit. All three Anthropic watchlist roles remain open: Institute Analyst, Communications Manager, and Geopolitics Analyst.

SpaceX IPO

SpaceX is preparing the largest IPO in history: $75 billion at a $1.75 trillion valuation. That's more than the entire US IPO market raised in 2024 and 2025 combined. Polymarket gives it an 89.5% chance of being the biggest IPO of the year. Musk recently merged XAI into SpaceX; all eleven XAI co-founders have left.

Tech & AI News

Anthropic's Claude Mythos leak continues. Axios reports the company is privately warning government officials that the model "presages an upcoming wave of models that can exploit vulnerabilities in ways that far outpace the efforts of defenders." Claude Mythos Leak → Pentagon DOD Claude Court Ruling →

The FTC chairman called media companies demanding payment for AI training data "rent-seeking" and "collecting tolls" — effectively giving AI companies a green light to train on news content.

NASA's Artemis crew entered quarantine ahead of a potential Wednesday launch. This would be the first crewed trip around the moon since 1972.

Your Day

Ben arrives today to help with carpet installation (scheduled for tomorrow at 9 AM). Payday is also tomorrow. Wednesday: Apple's 50th anniversary, Q2 starts, and Karen announces her retirement at 11 AM. Thursday is loaded: Anthropic pilot discussion at 9 AM, Bill Review Training at 2 PM, dinner with Alex, Chris, and Dustin at 6 PM, and the government appeal deadline coincides with your own workplace pilot meeting.

A comprehensive guide to local government in Jefferson City and Cole County, Missouri. Covers city council, county commission, school district, budgets, housing crisis, infrastructure challenges, economic development, upcoming elections, and how to stay civically engaged — all focused exclusively on local government, not state politics.
00:00:00 Introduction 00:01:00 The City at a Glance 00:02:52 How the City Government Works 00:05:35 How Cole County Government Works 00:07:46 Jefferson City Public Schools 00:09:49 The Big Issues 00:14:02 Policy Shifts & What's Changing 00:15:17 The Big Projects Coming 00:17:08 How to Stay Informed 00:18:38 The Interconnected Picture
item.content:encoded:
Know Your City: Jefferson City & Cole County Local Government

A deep dive into the local government, economy, and civic life of Jefferson City and Cole County. This episode covers everything you need to know as a resident — from who sits on city council to how the county budget works to what major projects are coming in the next five years.

City Government

Mayor Ron Fitzwater leads a 10-member city council (2 per ward, 5 wards) with City Administrator Brian Crane managing day-to-day operations. City budget: $86.8M. Council meets 1st and 3rd Monday at 6 PM. City Council Members →

Cole County

Three-member commission: Presiding Commissioner Sam Bushman, Jeff Hoelscher (East), Harry Otto (West). Operating budget: $64.4M plus $32.8M capital. Meets Tuesdays at 9 AM. County Commission →

Schools

JCPS serves 8,560 students across 18 schools. Academic scores improving three consecutive years. $20M athletic facilities expansion underway. JCPS Website →

Key Issues

Housing Crisis: Ranked 12th tightest market nationally post-tornado. Mayor targeting 50,000 residents (needs 3,000-5,000 new units). Housing Incentive →

High Street Viaduct: Emergency closure Feb 2026. $16-25M replacement planned, federal BUILD grant applied for. Viaduct Closure →

Downtown Conference Center: 189,420 sq ft mixed-use with 200-room hotel. Construction starting 2026, completion ~2028. $584M projected economic impact over a decade. Conference Center →

Property Tax Assessment: Cole County assessor failed state compliance. New assessor Tim Theroff took over Sept 2025. Assessment Issues →

Upcoming

April 2026 Election: 5 city council seats, 3 school board seats, and Cole County capital improvement sales tax renewal (extending to 10-year) all on the ballot.

Stay Informed

  • City Council Agendas & Video
  • County Commission Agenda Center
  • School Board Schedule
  • News Tribune - Local Coverage

item.title: Chapter One — The Letter (The Keeping House)
item.description:
Chapter One of The Keeping House — an original novel written one chapter at a time. Margot Ainsley arrives at a mysterious rural estate to begin a year-long residency at an institution whose purpose no one can explain.
00:00:00 Chapter One: The Letter 00:01:10 The House 00:03:39 The Library 00:05:07 Meeting June 00:07:01 The Cottage 00:08:47 The Hilltop 00:11:08 Evening 00:13:11 Night
item.content:encoded:
The Keeping House — Chapter One: The Letter

Margot Ainsley arrives a day early at The Keeping House, a rural estate turned residential retreat endowed by the late Eleanora Voss. She explores the house, meets the caretaker June, settles into her cottage, and watches the light move across a river valley from a hilltop bench. In the night, she wakes to the sound of another car on the gravel drive.

About the Novel

The Keeping House is an original literary novel being written one chapter per session. It follows six people who arrive at a mysterious rural retreat and must learn — against their own resistance — to stop performing and start being present.

item.title: Deep Dive — AI 2027 Report: Reality Check (Kokoro TTS Version)
item.description:
Same deep dive into the AI 2027 forecasting report — re-rendered with Kokoro TTS for A/B voice comparison. How well have Daniel Kokotajlo's predictions held up? What did the authors revise? What has 2026 reality looked like compared to the forecast?
00:00:00 Introduction & Report Overview 00:01:30 The Core Thesis 00:03:15 The Authors' Own Revision 00:05:00 What They Got Right 00:07:30 Mixed Results 00:09:00 Where They Missed 00:10:00 The DeepSeek Surprise 00:11:30 Overall Grade & Opinion
Note: This is the same episode as "Deep Dive — AI 2027 Report: Reality Check" re-rendered using Kokoro TTS for voice quality comparison.
item.content:encoded:
AI 2027 Report: Reality Check (Kokoro TTS Version)

A deep analysis of the AI 2027 forecasting report, examining predictions vs. 2026 reality. This version rendered with Kokoro TTS for A/B voice comparison against the original.

What They Got Right

Agent development trajectory, coding AI as leading indicator, government remaining behind the curve, and safety risks becoming visible — all largely validated. Read the original report →

What They Got Wrong

Timeline (authors revised from 2027 to ~2030), competitive dynamics (DeepSeek undermined compute-concentration thesis), China prediction (private enterprise, not nationalization), smooth scaling thesis.

The DeepSeek Surprise

An under-resourced Chinese startup training frontier models for ~$6M was not in anyone's scenario planning.

Overall Grade

B- for direction, D for timing. The what is right even if the when is wrong.

Kokoro TTS re-render — bonus episode for voice quality A/B comparison.

item.title: Week in Review — March 28, 2026
item.description:
Weekly briefing covering the week of March 22–28, 2026. Anthropic wins its landmark court case, Iran war escalates with Houthi entry, DHS shutdown worsens, and the week ahead is loaded.
00:00:00 Opening — The Week That Was 00:02:00 Highlights and Wins 00:03:15 Stories That Evolved 00:07:30 Vault Activity 00:08:30 Relationship Check 00:09:45 The Week Ahead 00:11:00 What to Watch 00:12:00 Closing
item.content:encoded:
The Week That Was

This was Anthropic's week — a 43-page federal injunction, a leaked next-gen model (Claude Mythos), iOS 27 Siri integration, and $19B ARR. Meanwhile, the Iran war escalated: Houthis entered the conflict, oil hit $112.57, and Congress left for recess while 61,000 TSA workers remain unpaid.

Anthropic v. Pentagon

Judge Rita Lin issued a preliminary injunction calling the Pentagon's designation "classic illegal First Amendment retaliation" and "Orwellian." Federal agencies can resume using Claude. The government has until April 2 to appeal. CNBC coverage →

Iran War — Days 23–29

The week opened with a 48-hour ultimatum and ended with Houthis firing on Israel. Oil surged from ~$102 to $112.57. Goldman Sachs says prices could stay elevated through 2027. The IEA called it worse than the 1970s oil shocks. Axios: Vance takes lead →

DHS Shutdown — Days 40–43

Senate passed bipartisan funding at 2:20 AM Friday. House rejected it, passed their own bill, then both chambers left for two-week recess. No resolution before mid-April.

Apple

iOS 26.4 dropped with Playlist Playground and new emoji. AirPods Max 2 pre-orders opened. WWDC confirmed June 8. Bloomberg: iOS 27 will open Siri to Claude, Gemini via Extensions system. Apple turns 50 on Wednesday.

The Week Ahead

Ben arrives Monday for carpet install Tuesday. Karen's retirement announcement Wednesday (Q2 starts). Anthropic pilot discussion Thursday morning — same day as the government's appeal deadline and dinner with friends.

item.title: Deep Dive — Claude Mythos: Anthropic's Biggest Leak and IPO Plans
item.description: A deep dive into the Claude Mythos leak — Anthropic's new Capybara-tier model with unprecedented cybersecurity capabilities, the Wall Street selloff it triggered, and the October 2026 IPO plans revealed the same day.
item.content:encoded:
The Leak

On March 26, 2026, a CMS misconfiguration at Anthropic exposed roughly 3,000 unpublished blog assets — including a draft post describing Claude Mythos, a new model tier above Opus. Security researchers Roy Paz (LayerX) and Alexandre Pauwels (Cambridge) found the exposed data store. Fortune broke the story.

What Is Capybara?

Capybara is Anthropic's new fourth model tier, positioned above Opus. Claude Mythos is the first model in this tier. Anthropic calls it "a step change" and "the most capable we've built to date," with dramatically higher scores in coding, reasoning, and cybersecurity.

Cybersecurity Concerns

The draft warns Mythos is "far ahead of any other AI model in cyber capabilities" — able to find and exploit software vulnerabilities at machine speed. This triggered a major cybersecurity stock selloff: CrowdStrike -7%, Palo Alto Networks -6%, Tenable -11%, Zscaler -4.5%.

IPO Plans

Bloomberg reported the same day that Anthropic is targeting an October 2026 IPO, potentially raising 0B+. The company is valued at ~80B after a 0B February funding round, running at 4B annualized revenue. The timing has fueled skepticism about whether the leak was truly accidental.

Open Questions

When will Mythos be available via API? What will pricing look like? Will cybersecurity concerns trigger regulation? And was this leak intentional pre-IPO marketing?

item.title: Daily Briefing — Saturday Evening Update, March 28, 2026
item.description: Trump extends Hormuz deadline 10 days. Dubai crude hits $126. Iranian Red Crescent reports 1,900 killed. TSA paychecks may arrive Monday. Bank of America settles Epstein lawsuit for $72.5M. Anthropic nears $19B annualized revenue. Apple partners with Google Gemini for Siri overhaul.
item.content:encoded:
Iran War — Evening Update

Trump extended the Strait of Hormuz deadline by 10 days — Iran now has until ~April 7 to reopen the strait. Dubai crude benchmark at $126/barrel, up 76% since the war started. Pakistan now acting as intermediary. Iranian Red Crescent: 1,900 killed since Feb 28. In Lebanon, 1,142 killed including 122 children.

DHS Shutdown

TSA workers should start seeing paychecks as early as Monday, March 30.

Financial

Bank of America agreed to pay $72.5M to settle Epstein-related lawsuit. Anthropic approaching $19B annualized revenue.

Apple

Apple partnering with Google to use 1.2T parameter Gemini model for reimagined Siri in iOS 26.4.

item.title: Daily Briefing — Saturday Mid-Morning Update, March 28, 2026
item.description: Houthis enter Iran war. 3,100+ No Kings protests planned. Warhorse Studios fires translator for AI. Apple turns 50 Wednesday. Cherry blossoms in Tacoma.
item.content:encoded:
Weather

Jefferson City: frost at dawn (31°F), climbing to 83°F with full sun. Tacoma: 56°F, partly cloudy, rain tomorrow.

Texts

Katelyn asking about bird sightings — you two have been trading all week. Alex sent The Verge's Apple 50th anniversary product ranking at 11 PM Friday.

Iran War Escalation

Houthis launched their first military operation in support of Iran — a ballistic missile at Israel. Debris caused fires at a UAE industrial hub. Drone hit Kuwait airport radar. The 10-day Hormuz extension is the framework but the situation is more fragile.

No Kings Protests

3,100+ demonstrations planned nationwide, organizers expecting millions. Two-thirds of RSVPs from outside major cities, up 40% from June 2025.

DHS Shutdown Day 43

Congress left for two-week recess without a deal. 61,000 TSA workers have missed three paychecks.

AI Replacement Watch

Warhorse Studios (Kingdom Come Deliverance 2) fired their Czech-to-English translator and replaced him with AI. No warning. 12K upvotes, 1,500 comments on Reddit.

Apple

Turns 50 Wednesday. iPhone Air C1X modem matches Qualcomm on speed, wins on latency. 15+ new products expected this year. No iPhone in Lockdown Mode has ever been hacked.

From Your Inbox

Claude Team: Claude Code can now keep working while you're away. 404 Media: Earth's magnetic field creating cosmic ray void. The Atlantic: America sliding toward illiteracy.

Tacoma

Cherry blossoms blooming at Wapato Hills — gorgeous photos on r/Tacoma.

item.title: Daily Briefing — Saturday, March 28, 2026
item.description: Houthis enter the Iran war with missile strikes on Israel. Ten US service members injured at Prince Sultan Air Base. Brent crude hits $112.57. DHS shutdown day 43 — Congress leaves on recess. Anthropic appeal deadline Thursday. WA bans non-competes. Apple turns 50 Wednesday. Your week ahead.
item.content:encoded:
Iran War — Day 29

The Houthis entered the war, launching ballistic missiles at Israel — their first strike since the Hamas ceasefire. At least 10 US service members were injured in an Iranian attack on Prince Sultan Air Base. Brent crude hit $112.57, up 4.2%. Reddit surfaced a WSJ report that the US may deploy up to 17,000 troops near Iran. The UK’s Starmer reaffirmed Britain will not join.

DHS Shutdown — Day 43

The Senate passed a bipartisan funding bill, then the House rejected it and passed their own (213-203), which Senate Democrats called dead on arrival. Both chambers left for a two-week recess. 61,000+ TSA workers have missed three paychecks.

Anthropic v. Pentagon

Judge Lin’s preliminary injunction stands. Government appeal deadline is Thursday, April 2 — the same day as Jacob’s Anthropic pilot discussion at work.

Tech & AI

Semafor: A 19-year-old’s AI agent team spent £1,426 on unauthorized catering. Stratechery: RIP Sora, OpenAI killed it for GPUs. Kash Patel’s email was hacked. SoftBank taking a $40B loan for OpenAI (IPO signal). WA bans non-compete agreements.

Apple

Apple turns 50 on Wednesday. No iPhone in Lockdown Mode has ever been hacked. The C1X modem matches Qualcomm on speed.

Calendar

Monday: Ben arrives. Tuesday: Payday + carpet install. Wednesday: Q2 starts, Karen retirement announcement, Apple 50th. Thursday: Anthropic pilot 9AM, dinner with friends 6PM, government appeal deadline.

From Reddit

r/Tacoma: Cherry blossoms at Wapato Hills, No Kings protest today. r/SeattleWA: WA bans non-competes, Seattle police starting pay ~$118K. r/apple: Lockdown Mode undefeated, Apple hiring Google VP for AI.
